title: Kirkley (Lowestoft) Halifax Memorial
crew_summary: 7 killed; pilot Flt Sgt. George H. Williamson (RAAF)
memorial_type: Bowls club memorial seat with plaque

sources.title: Roll of Honour - Tempsford Halifax LL356
MHHV - A Special Halifax Bomber Crew

narrative: On 27 April 1944, Halifax LL356 of the highly secretive 138 (Special Duties) Squadron crashed at Kirkley, a suburb of Lowestoft on the Suffolk coast. No. 138 Squadron was one of RAF's clandestine units, based at Tempsford in Bedfordshire, tasked with dropping agents, supplies, and equipment to resistance movements across occupied Europe. Their operations were hazardous and rarely publicised. The circumstances of this crash -- with the aircraft coming down on the Suffolk coast, far from its Bedfordshire base -- suggest the Halifax may have been returning from or departing for a special operations mission. The pilot, Flight Sergeant George Herbert Williamson of the Royal Australian Air Force, and all six other crew members were killed.

crew.name: George Herbert Williamson
crew.fate_detail: Body washed up on the Dutch island of Terschelling on 16 June 1944. Buried there in a Commonwealth War Grave.
crew.bio: Australian pilot serving with the RAAF, aged 29 at the time of his death. He flew special operations missions dropping supplies to resistance movements in occupied Europe.

crew.name: George Partridge Croad
crew.fate_detail: Body never recovered from the North Sea. Commemorated on the Runnymede Memorial, Panel 227.
crew.bio: The youngest crew member at just 19 years old, serving with the RAFVR as flight engineer on clandestine special operations missions.

crew.name: Herbert Dootson
crew.fate_detail: Body never recovered from the North Sea. Commemorated on the Runnymede Memorial, Panel 217.
crew.bio: At 33 years old, Dootson was the oldest crew member and an experienced navigator serving with the RAFVR.

crew.name: Arthur John George Barnes
crew.fate_detail: Body never recovered from the North Sea. Commemorated on the Runnymede Memorial, Panel 254.
crew.bio: Canadian airman serving with the RCAF, aged 22. One of two Canadians in the multinational crew.

crew.name: Hubert Francis Benbow
crew.fate_detail: Body never recovered from the North Sea. Commemorated on the Runnymede Memorial, Panel 225.
crew.bio: Served with the RAFVR as wireless operator and air gunner on special duties operations.

crew.name: Eric Raymond Clayworth
crew.fate_detail: Body never recovered from the North Sea. Commemorated on the Runnymede Memorial, Panel 260.
crew.bio: Australian airman serving with the RAAF, aged 25. One of two Australians aboard the aircraft.

crew.name: James Ethelred Smythe
crew.fate_detail: Body never recovered from the North Sea. Commemorated on the Runnymede Memorial, Panel 255.
crew.bio: Canadian airman serving with the RCAF, the youngest at 21. The crew represented four nations: Australia, Canada, and Britain.

cause_of_loss: Hit by German flak over Eindhoven, Holland, while on mission to drop supplies to Belgian resistance. Aircraft listed as Missing on Operations.
memorial_details: | Field | Detail | |-------|--------| | Type | Plaque on memorial seat | | Location | Kirkley Bowls Club, Lowestoft, Suffolk | | Access | Publicly accessible |

external_narratives.summary: Detailed page on F/Sgt George Herbert Williamson RAAF (aged 29) and the crew of Halifax V LL356 (coded NF-U). The aircraft departed Tempsford at 23:10 on 27 April 1944 to drop supplies to Belgian resistance. It was observed being hit by German flak over Eindhoven in southern Holland. The crew likely attempted to return to base but crashed or ditched, with the aircraft listed as 'Missing on Operations'. The crew comprised airmen from the RAAF, RAFVR, and RCAF.
key_details: Mission to drop supplies to Belgian resistance
Hit by flak over Eindhoven, Holland

external_narratives.summary: Australian military heritage article documenting the crew of Halifax LL356 as a 'Special' Halifax bomber crew from 138 Squadron. Provides context on the squadron's clandestine SOE operations and the multinational nature of the crew.
ASN records the accident of Halifax Mk V LL356 on 28 April 1944, confirming loss details and crew complement.

The Roll of Honour website lists all seven crew members of Halifax LL356 NF-U who were killed on the special operations mission. A memorial seat with plaque was dedicated at Kirkley Bowls Club, Lowestoft, with support from the RAFA Lowestoft Branch.
key_details: All seven crew listed by name

local_contacts.role: Local authority responsible for memorial upkeep
